epigraph.text:
A just balance and scales are the Lord's; all the 
weights in the bag are his work.

content.text:
An equation is like a balanced scale. The equals sign tells us that 
what's on the left has the same value as what's on the right. Understanding 
this balance is the key to solving equations.
It's important to distinguish between expressions and equations:

content.title: Equation
content.content:
An **equation** is a mathematical statement that 
two expressions are equal. It always contains an equals sign (=).

content.text:
An expression can be simplified, but it cannot be "solved." An equation 
can be solved because it makes a claim about equality that can be tested.

content.title: Solution of an Equation
content.content:
A **solution** of an equation is a value that 
makes the equation true when substituted for the variable.

content.title: Checking solutions
content.solution:
Substitute $x = 5$ into the equation:
\[
3(5) + 2 = 15 + 2 = 17 \checkmark
\]
Yes, $x = 5$ is a solution because $17 = 17$ is true.

content.title: Testing values
content.problem: Which value is a solution of $2x - 3 = 7$: $x = 4$ or $x = 5$?
content.solution:
Test $x = 4$: $2(4) - 3 = 8 - 3 = 5 \neq 7$ ✗

Test $x = 5$: $2(5) - 3 = 10 - 3 = 7$ 

Therefore, $x = 5$ is the solution.

content.content:
Just as there is one truth that satisfies the requirements of an equation, 
Christ declared, "I am the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6). 
Finding the solution to an equation is like discovering the one truth 
that makes everything balance.

content.text:
Think of an equation as a balance scale. To keep it balanced, whatever 
you do to one side, you must do to the other.

content.title: Balance Principle
content.content:
When solving equations, you can:

- Add the same number to both sides

- Subtract the same number from both sides

- Multiply both sides by the same nonzero number

- Divide both sides by the same nonzero number
These operations preserve equality.

content.title: Balance demonstration
content.solution:
Apply operations to both sides:
$$
5 &= 5 && \text{Original true statement} \\
5 + 3 &= 5 + 3 \Rightarrow 8 = 8 && \text{Add 3 to both sides} \\
5 \times 2 &= 5 \times 2 \Rightarrow 10 = 10 && \text{Multiply both sides by 2}
$$
The equality is preserved because we treated both sides the same way.

content.text:
To isolate a variable, we use **inverse operations**—operations 
that undo each other:

content.title: Using inverse operations
content.problem: If you add 5 to a number and get 12, what was the original number?
content.solution:
To undo "add 5," we subtract 5:
\[
12 - 5 = 7
\]
The original number was 7. Check: $7 + 5 = 12$

content.title: Scripture
content.content:
In Galatians 6:7, Paul writes, "A man reaps what he sows." This 
principle of cause and effect parallels inverse operations: sowing 
and reaping are connected, just as addition and subtraction are 
inverses. Every mathematical operation has a way to be reversed.

marginNotes.text:
The Lord detests dishonest scales, but 
accurate weights find favor with him.

You are to use accurate scales, an 
accurate ephah and an accurate bath.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
Mathematicians have tried in vain to 
discover some order in the sequence of prime numbers.

marginNotes.author: George Pólya
marginNotes.text:
If you can't solve a problem, then there is 
an easier problem you can solve: find it.
